apis.name: backgroundExtensionEffect()
MenuBarExtra (SwiftUI Scene)
MenuBarExtra window background & Liquid Glass
MenuBarExtra refresh patterns (open vs closed)
SMAppService.mainApp (launch at login)
LSUIElement + NSApp activation policy
Manual .app bundle from SwiftPM (no Xcode project)
apis.usage:
# Directory layout:
# MyApp.app/
#   Contents/
#     Info.plist
#     MacOS/
#       MyApp          <- swift build output
#     Resources/       <- optional: AppIcon.icns, assets

# Makefile target:
APP   = MyApp.app
BIN   = .build/release/MyApp
INFO  = Resources/Info.plist

build:
	swift build -c release
	mkdir -p $(APP)/Contents/MacOS
	mkdir -p $(APP)/Contents/Resources
	cp $(BIN) $(APP)/Contents/MacOS/MyApp
	cp $(INFO) $(APP)/Contents/Info.plist
	# Ad-hoc signing for local distribution (no Apple ID required):
	codesign --force --deep --sign - $(APP)

# Minimum Info.plist for a SwiftUI MenuBarExtra app:
# <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
# <!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN"
#   "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
# <plist version="1.0"><dict>
#   <key>CFBundleIdentifier</key><string>com.example.MyApp</string>
#   <key>CFBundleName</key><string>MyApp</string>
#   <key>CFBundleExecutable</key><string>MyApp</string>
#   <key>CFBundleVersion</key><string>1</string>
#   <key>CFBundleShortVersionString</key><string>1.0</string>
#   <key>LSMinimumSystemVersion</key><string>13.0</string>
#   <key>NSPrincipalClass</key><string>NSApplication</string>
#   <key>NSHighResolutionCapable</key><true/>
#   <key>LSUIElement</key><true/>
# </dict></plist>

# Third-party alternative (more reliable, handles entitlements):
# mint install stackotter/swift-bundler@main
# swift bundler create MyMenuBarApp --template SwiftUI
# swift bundler run

# Verdict 2026: generating an Xcode project (via xcodegen or
# swift bundler generate-xcode-support) is more reliable because:
# - 'swift package generate-xcodeproj' is deprecated
# - Xcode handles entitlements, signing, and Info.plist injection
# - swift-bundler is the best headless alternative (~500 stars,
#   actively maintained, 48h critical bug SLA)

menubar_patterns:
Use .menuBarExtraStyle(.window) for any popover that contains custom SwiftUI layout beyond simple menu items. Size is entirely driven by .frame() on the root content view — there is no separate window size API. A 280-360 pt wide, up to 500 pt tall panel is typical; keep height under the screen height minus menu bar height or the panel clips.

Window background: the .window style gives you a plain borderless NSPanel with no automatic material in macOS 13-25. In macOS 26 the system still does not inject Liquid Glass automatically into a MenuBarExtra panel the way it does for sheets and navigation bars. Best practice:
- macOS 13-25 target: apply .background(.regularMaterial) to the root content view
- macOS 26 target: wrap the root view in a ZStack with a Rectangle().glassEffect(in: Rectangle()) at the bottom layer and ignoresSafeArea(); or apply .glassEffect() selectively to chrome controls inside the panel
- Never add both a .background() material AND a .glassEffect() — you will get a double-glass artefact

Refresh patterns: the .window style reliably fires onAppear when the popover opens and onDisappear when it closes. Attach a Timer.publish autoconnect sink in onAppear and cancel in onDisappear for "live while open" data. For background polling (e.g., checking system state every 30 s regardless of visibility), maintain a shared @Observable model that drives a background timer; the popover reads from it reactively.

Dismissing programmatically from within the popover content: use @Environment(\.dismiss) private var dismiss and call dismiss(). This is preferable to toggling the isInserted binding from a distant location. For external dismissal (e.g., after a hotkey), hold a Bool @State at the @main App level and use MenuBarExtra(isInserted: $shown) { ... }.

Launch at login: import ServiceManagement and call try SMAppService.mainApp.register() / .unregister(). Check .status before writing to avoid redundant state transitions. If status is .requiresApproval, call SMAppService.openSystemSettingsLoginItems() to guide the user. No entitlement is required for mainApp on a non-sandboxed app.

Suppress Dock icon: set LSUIElement = true in Info.plist. A SwiftUI @main struct that declares only a MenuBarExtra scene adopts .accessory activation policy automatically in macOS 13+, but including LSUIElement prevents a one-frame Dock icon flash during launch.
hig_notes:
Menu bar extras (status items) occupy shared, limited real estate. Apple HIG rules:

Icons: always use a template image (monochromatic, uses transparency/alpha channel). SF Symbols are the preferred source — they are automatically template-rendered, scale with Dynamic Type, and adapt to Light/Dark menu bar appearance. Recommended icon size is 18×18 pt (the button area is ~28 pt). Never use full-color images for the status item button itself.

When to show text next to the icon: only when displaying dynamic glanceable data the user needs at a glance without clicking — CPU percentage, clock, battery level, network speed. Keep it as short as possible (< 6 characters). For a static label that names the app, omit text and use icon-only.

When to add a menu bar extra at all: only for apps that provide persistent, always-accessible utility (VPN status, clipboard manager, system monitor). Do not add one simply to give an app an alternative launch path — use the Dock or Spotlight for that.

Popover content density: keep the popover focused on the single primary function of the app. Avoid recreating a full app window inside the popover. Prefer concise controls. macOS HIG prefers small/mini control sizes (.controlSize(.small)) in dense menu-extra panels. Avoid deep navigation hierarchies inside the popover.

Liquid Glass and the content vs controls layer distinction (macOS 26 HIG): Liquid Glass belongs to the navigation/chrome layer — toolbars, floating controls, menus, sidebars. It must NOT be applied to the content layer — list rows, table cells, cards in a scroll view. In a MenuBarExtra popover, apply glass only to toolbar-like headers, floating action buttons, or grouped control pills at the top/bottom of the panel. Leave the scrollable content area with a plain or material background, not glass.
pitfalls:
1. Glass on glass stacking: placing .glassEffect() inside another .glassEffect() view is invalid — glass cannot sample other glass. Always wrap sibling glass views in a GlassEffectContainer. Without the container you get visible seams and incorrect refraction at element boundaries.

2. macOS vs iOS differences: the .interactive() modifier on GlassEffect (bounce + shimmer on press) is primarily iOS behaviour. On macOS it is a no-op or nearly so — the glass press feedback on macOS follows the system's standard hover/press highlighting. Do not design interactions that depend on the iOS interactive glass animation on macOS.

3. macOS 26 Liquid Glass is subtler than iOS 26: on macOS the lensing and refraction are toned down to suit desktop context, matching the historically more conservative vibrancy behaviour. Over flat grey or uniform backgrounds, glass provides almost no visual effect. It shines only when rich photo or colorful content scrolls beneath it. Test over a real photo wallpaper, not the default grey desktop.

4. Legibility: do not place dense body text directly on a glass surface. Glass adapts tint and shadow dynamically but cannot guarantee contrast ratios. Use .foregroundStyle(.primary) rather than hardcoded colors. For critical readable text (menus, labels), place it on a solid or frosted fill instead of bare glass.

5. GlassEffectContainer is a performance requirement, not a suggestion: each standalone .glassEffect() view is an independent compositing pass. A panel with 5 standalone glass buttons means 5 passes per frame. Wrap them in one GlassEffectContainer for a single batched pass.

6. Reduced Transparency accessibility: always test with System Settings > Accessibility > Display > Reduce Transparency enabled. Glass degrades gracefully (the system makes it frostier and more opaque), but if you have hardcoded layout that assumes a transparent background you will have visual regressions. Use @Environment(\.accessibilityReduceTransparency) to adapt if needed.

7. Fallback for macOS < 26: glassEffect is unavailable before macOS 26. Guard with #available(macOS 26.0, *) and fall back to .background(.regularMaterial, in: shape). Using the same shape in both branches makes the visual difference minimal.

8. SwiftPM executable targets cannot produce .app bundles natively: 'swift build' outputs a flat Unix executable, not a bundle. 'swift package generate-xcodeproj' is deprecated as of Xcode 15 and removed in Xcode 26. The two reliable paths are (a) use swift-bundler (stackotter/swift-bundler) which handles Info.plist injection, icon sets, and signing from a Package.swift manifest, or (b) generate an Xcode project with xcodegen from a project.yml. The manual Makefile approach (copy binary + write Info.plist + codesign --sign -) works for local ad-hoc use but breaks on any entitlement (hardened runtime, sandbox) because entitlements require a provisioning profile or Developer ID.

9. Ad-hoc signing (codesign --force --deep --sign -): sufficient for running the .app on the same machine without an Apple Developer account. It will not pass Gatekeeper on other machines (users get "unidentified developer" quarantine). For distribution beyond your own machine, you need a Developer ID certificate and notarization even outside the App Store.

10. NSPrincipalClass must be NSApplication: in a manually constructed Info.plist for a SwiftUI @main App, include <key>NSPrincipalClass</key><string>NSApplication</string>. Omitting it causes the process to launch but shows no windows and exhibits undefined activation behaviour.

11. CFBundleExecutable must match the binary filename exactly (case-sensitive). A mismatch causes launch failure with a cryptic "killed 9" or "exec format error" in Console.

12. Do not use glass for the content layer in a MenuBarExtra popover: if your popover contains a List or scrollable rows, those rows must use plain fills, vibrancy, or .ultraThinMaterial backgrounds — never .glassEffect(). Applying glass to list cells causes extreme per-frame GPU cost and visual clutter that Apple explicitly calls out as incorrect in the "Meet Liquid Glass" and "Get to know the new design system" WWDC25 sessions.
